text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Malhotra  
Northgate Health Centre  
14 Northgate Road, London NW1 8AL  

Dear Dr Priya Malhotra,  

Alex Martin, DOB: 22-11-1988, Hospital No: U728415 NHS No. 4093276158  
Flat 3, 5 Arbour Lane, London, E3 4QH 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Focal epilepsy following probable left temporal lobe onset; history of cold-chain warehouse employment with prolonged low-temperature exposure; mild anxiety symptoms; smoker; no alcohol excess reported. 

Medication: Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily. 
Lacosamide 100 mg twice daily. 
Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily. 
Omeprazole 20 mg once daily. 

I reviewed Alex in clinic today. He works in a cold-chain warehouse where ambient temperatures are routinely below −20°C. He noted that thick thermal gloves and the need to work rapidly with handheld scanners occasionally make accessing his rescue medication pouch slower than ideal. He brought photos of his workstation (labels partly garbled on his phone: Ã‚Â·â€˜Dock Bâ€™, ï¿½Freezer 3) to illustrate the set-up. He lives with his partner and commutes by bus. Sleep has been variable due to early shifts, but he denies missed doses over the past month. Appetite is normal and weight is stable. No intercurrent illness. 

Seizures: Alex has focal epilepsy with probable temporal lobe onset, characterised by brief déjà vu, epigastric rising sensation, and impaired awareness lasting 1–2 minutes, occasionally progressing to right-hand automatisms; recovery within 10–15 minutes with post-ictal fatigue. He and his partner usually track events in a phone diary. Cluster frequency unclear this month; last month ≈three clusters. He attributes potential under-recording this month to shift changes and a faulty app sync (â€˜last backup failedâ€™ icon appeared). No generalised tonic–clonic seizures reported in the past six months. He reports cold exposure and high cognitive load during stock reconciliation as potential precipitating factors. He keeps buccal midazolam in a belt pouch but notes that glove removal in the freezer aisles can slow deployment by ~30–60 seconds. No injuries reported; one near-fall on an icy loading bay (Ã©) without head impact. 

Examination: Alert and oriented; speech fluent; cranial nerves intact; no focal motor deficit; coordination and gait normal. No skin breakdown from cold exposure on inspection of hands. Vitals within normal range. 

Investigations: Last MRI (2023) non-lesional. Prior EEG with left temporal intermittent rhythmic delta activity; interictal spikes rare. Routine bloods (July 2025) normal including LFTs and U&Es; vitamin D adequate. 

Plan: 
- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily and lacosamide 100 mg twice daily; no dose change today given current tolerability and absence of recent generalised events. 
- Reinforce adherence and diary use; advised switching to paper backup if the phone app fails (ï¿½ sync issues). 
- Occupational context: we discussed rescue access in low temperatures; Alex will trial a thin inner glove under removable outer gloves and reposition the midazolam pouch to a chest pocket for quicker reach. 
- Provide employer letter confirming focal epilepsy, typical semiology, and need for rapid access to rescue medication; no night driving; continue existing workplace risk assessment (no work at unguarded heights or lone working in freezer aisles). 
- Epilepsy nurse to review seizure logging in 6 weeks and verify cluster pattern. 
- Safety: avoid sleep deprivation; maintain regular meals and hydration; continue smoking cessation advice. 
- Follow-up in 4 months, sooner if clusters increase or any generalised tonic–clonic seizure occurs. 

Yours sincerely

attributes.Label: 3 cluster per month, multiple per cluster

entities.text: Cluster frequency unclear this month; last month ≈three clusters

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "last month ≈three clusters." The current month’s cluster frequency is unclear, so the most concrete numeric frequency is last month’s. No per-cluster seizure count is given. Therefore, use the cluster format with 'multiple per cluster' to reflect unspecified counts.
seizure_frequency_number: 3 cluster per month, multiple per cluster
